section.title: A Cry for Mercy in Distress
section.sectionRationale: The first of the traditional penitential psalms, a raw lament from physical and emotional agony that turns, without any narrated resolution, into sudden confidence that the LORD has heard.
section.thesis: David, weak in body and flooded with weeping, asks God not to rebuke him in anger but to heal and deliver him for the sake of His loving devotion, and even before his circumstances change, declares that the LORD has heard his cry for mercy.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the opening cry from bodily and emotional agony (vv1-3), the plea for deliverance grounded in God's loving devotion (vv4-7), and the sudden turn to confidence that the LORD has heard, putting his enemies to shame (vv8-10).

units.label: A cry from agony
units.note: how long, O LORD, how long

verses.text: O LORD, do not rebuke me in Your anger or discipline me in Your wrath.
Be merciful to me, O LORD, for I am frail; heal me, O LORD, for my bones are in agony.
My soul is deeply distressed. How long, O LORD, how long?

units.label: Deliver me for Your loving devotion
units.note: no mention of You in death

verses.text: Turn, O LORD, and deliver my soul; save me because of Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}.
For there is no mention of You in death; who can praise You from Sheol?
I am weary from groaning; all night I flood my bed with weeping and drench my couch with tears.
My eyes fail from grief; they grow dim because of all my foes.

units.label: The LORD has heard
units.note: depart, all you workers of iniquity

verses.text: Depart from me, all you workers of iniquity, for the LORD has heard my weeping.
The LORD has heard my cry for mercy; the LORD accepts my prayer.
All my enemies will be ashamed and dismayed; they will turn back in sudden disgrace.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast love, loyal covenant devotion. David appeals to this, not his own innocence, as the reason God should save him, showing that even in deep affliction the ground of hope is God's committed character rather than the sufferer's merit.

basic.context: Psalm 6 is the first of the seven traditional penitential psalms, a raw cry from physical weakness and overwhelming grief. David asks God not to discipline him in anger but to heal him because of His loving devotion, and, remarkably, without any narrated change in circumstances, declares partway through that the LORD has heard his weeping.
basic.hermeneutics: This is honest lament prayer, not stoic resignation; the psalm gives full voice to physical agony, sleepless weeping, and fear of death, and models how genuine faith can hold together unresolved suffering with sudden, confident assurance that God has heard, even before anything visibly changes.
basic.originalLanguages: David appeals to God's chesed, His steadfast covenant love, as the reason to be saved, not his own innocence. His argument in verse 5, that the dead do not praise God from Sheol, is not a doctrine of the afterlife but an urgent appeal, as if to say, save me now, so that I may go on praising You.
basic.typology: David's cry from bodily agony and near despair, met by sudden assurance of being heard, anticipates Christ's own cries of anguish, met by the Father, and every believer's confidence that prayers offered in weakness are truly heard even before deliverance is seen.
basic.christ: Jesus, in Gethsemane, cried out in similarly deep anguish, His soul overwhelmed to the point of death, Matthew 26:38, and was heard by His Father, Hebrews 5:7, modeling exactly the pattern David lives out here: honest agony met by confident trust that God hears.
basic.god: God disciplines out of love, not mere anger, yet David can appeal past that discipline to God's own loving devotion, trusting that the same God who allows affliction is the one who will hear, heal, and turn his enemies back in shame.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 5:7 describes Christ's own loud cries and tears heard by the Father. Matthew 7:23 echoes the phrase 'depart from Me' in reverse, used later of Christ Himself rejecting workers of iniquity. Psalm 30:5 promises that weeping may last the night but joy comes in the morning.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 6 gives voice to the believer's darkest nights, bodily agony, sleepless grief, and fear of death, and teaches that even there, before anything changes, the LORD hears. Christ entered exactly this kind of anguish for us, and was heard, so that every cry of ours joined to His is certain to be heard as well.

context.literary: One of the seven traditional penitential psalms, structured around a striking shift from unresolved lament to sudden, unexplained confidence, a pattern found across many psalms that models faith holding grief and trust together.
context.historical: The superscription, for the choirmaster with stringed instruments, upon an eight stringed harp, likely indicates a particular tune or instrument used in temple worship for a psalm associated with sickness, deep distress, or approaching death.
context.canonical: Alongside Psalms 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, and 143, Psalm 6 became one of the church's classic penitential psalms, historically used in seasons of repentance and mourning.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give voice to genuine physical and emotional agony while modeling how appeal to God's own character, His loving devotion, rather than personal innocence, can produce sudden confidence that prayer has been heard, even before circumstances visibly change.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific bodily and emotional crisis; it prescribes for every sufferer the pattern of honest lament joined to appeal for mercy grounded in God's character, not a guarantee of the exact same sudden reversal in every case.

debates.question: Is this psalm evidence that David's suffering here was divine discipline for specific sin, making it a true penitential psalm?

views.label: Yes, discipline for sin
views.case: The opening plea, do not rebuke me in Your anger or discipline me in Your wrath, uses language elsewhere applied to God's correction of sin, and the psalm's traditional classification as penitential reflects this reading.

views.label: Suffering without a stated specific sin, akin to Job
views.case: The psalm never names a specific sin, unlike Psalm 51's explicit confession, so some read this as affliction, whether illness, persecution, or unspecified trial, met with a plea that God's discipline, whatever the cause, not be pressed in full wrath.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm's prayer works the same way: appeal to God's mercy and loving devotion rather than personal innocence, a pattern available to every sufferer regardless of the affliction's precise cause.

study.originalLanguages: David pleads for deliverance lema'an chasdecha, for the sake of Your loving devotion, appealing to God's covenant character rather than his own merit. His argument that 'there is no mention of You in death, who can praise You from Sheol' is a passionate rhetorical appeal for present rescue, common in the Psalms, rather than a settled doctrine denying any afterlife.

typology.type: The sufferer whose loud weeping and cries are heard by God, Psalm 6:8-9
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own loud cries and tears in the days of His flesh, heard by the Father who was able to save Him from death, Hebrews 5:7.

study.christ: David's anguish, bones in agony, soul deeply distressed, nights flooded with weeping, anticipates the greater anguish of Christ in Gethsemane, whose soul was overwhelmed to the point of death, Matthew 26:38, and who offered loud cries and tears to the One able to save Him, and was heard, Hebrews 5:7. Because Christ's cry was heard, every believer's cry joined to His, even from the depths of agony, is certain to reach the Father.
study.god: God is both the one whose discipline David fears and the one whose loving devotion David appeals to for rescue; He is not divided against Himself, but the psalm shows that even under felt divine displeasure, His character remains the believer's only sure ground of hope.

crossReferences.note: Describes Christ's own loud cries and tears offered to the Father, who heard Him, echoing the pattern of this psalm's anguished prayer.
Jesus in Gethsemane, His soul overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death, the deepest fulfillment of this psalm's agony.
Promises that weeping may last through the night but joy comes in the morning, the same turn from tears to confidence seen in Psalm 6.

study.oneStory: Psalm 6 gives every sufferer permission to bring bodily agony and sleepless grief honestly before God, and teaches that His loving devotion, not our own merit, is the ground for confident hope, even before anything visibly changes. Christ entered this same anguish, was heard by His Father, and now ensures that every cry joined to His reaches home.

quiz.q: What does David ask God NOT to do in verse 1?

options.text: Rebuke him in anger or discipline him in wrath.
options.note: This is the psalm's opening plea, asking for mercy rather than full wrath.

options.text: Answer his prayer at all.
options.note: David desperately wants to be heard; his plea is against full-force anger, not against being answered.

options.text: Heal his physical body.
options.note: David explicitly asks for healing in verse 2; his plea in verse 1 is against harsh discipline.

options.text: Reveal Himself to David's enemies.
options.note: This is not mentioned in verse 1 at all.

quiz.q: On what basis does David ask to be saved in verse 4?

options.text: For the sake of God's loving devotion, chesed, not his own merit.
options.note: David explicitly grounds his appeal in God's covenant character.

options.text: Because he has never sinned.
options.note: David does not claim sinlessness; he appeals to God's mercy and loving devotion instead.

options.text: Because his enemies are more wicked than he is.
options.note: The appeal is to God's own character, not a comparison of relative wickedness.

options.text: He gives no reason at all.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly states the reason: God's loving devotion.

quiz.q: How does David describe his nights in verse 6?

options.text: He is weary from groaning, flooding his bed with weeping and drenching his couch with tears all night.
options.note: This vividly pictures the depth of David's ongoing grief and exhaustion.

options.text: He sleeps peacefully despite his troubles.
options.note: This is the opposite of what verse 6 describes; his nights are marked by weeping, not peace.

options.text: He spends his nights planning revenge.
options.note: The verse describes grief and weeping, not scheming or plotting.

options.text: He does not mention his nights at all.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly describes his nightly weeping in vivid detail.

quiz.q: What sudden shift happens in verses 8-9?

options.text: David declares with confidence that the LORD has heard his weeping and his cry for mercy, even though his circumstances have not visibly changed.
options.note: This is the striking, unresolved turn from lament to confident assurance.

options.text: His enemies suddenly repent and become his friends.
options.note: The shift is in David's own confidence that he has been heard, not a reported change in his enemies' hearts.

options.text: God audibly speaks to David to explain the reason for his suffering.
options.note: No explanation is given; David simply declares his confidence that his prayer has been heard.

options.text: Nothing changes; the psalm ends exactly as it began.
options.note: Verses 8-9 mark a clear and dramatic shift in tone toward confidence.

quiz.q: How does this psalm connect to Christ's experience in Gethsemane?

options.text: Jesus also cried out with loud cries and tears from deep anguish, and was heard by His Father, Hebrews 5:7.
options.note: Hebrews 5:7 directly describes Christ's anguished, heard prayer, paralleling this psalm's pattern.

options.text: Jesus never experienced anguish comparable to this psalm.
options.note: Matthew 26:38 describes Jesus' soul as overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death, directly comparable anguish.

options.text: The connection is only that both figures prayed at some point.
options.note: The connection is much more specific: both offer anguished cries from deep distress that are heard by God.

options.text: There is no meaningful connection between this psalm and Christ.
options.note: Hebrews 5:7 draws a clear, specific parallel to this kind of anguished, heard prayer.

questions: David brings raw physical and emotional pain honestly to God rather than hiding it. What pain, physical or emotional, are you tempted to keep from God rather than bring openly to Him in prayer?

David appeals to God's loving devotion, not his own innocence, as the reason to be saved. Where are you tempted to believe God's help depends on your own performance rather than His steadfast character?

David's confidence that he has been heard comes before any change in his circumstances. Have you ever experienced this kind of assurance in prayer even while your situation remained the same? What did that feel like?
David flooded his bed with tears night after night; his grief was not quick or tidy. How do you make room for ongoing grief in your own life rather than rushing yourself toward resolution?
Jesus cried out in anguish in Gethsemane and was heard by His Father. How does knowing that Christ Himself prayed through deep anguish change the way you view your own desperate prayers?

translationNotes.note: Rebuke and discipline both describe corrective action, language elsewhere applied to God's fatherly correction of His people, Hebrews 12:5-6, though the psalm does not name a specific sin behind David's suffering.

There is no mention of You in death is a common rhetorical appeal found across the Psalms, urging God to act now so the psalmist can continue praising Him, rather than a settled doctrinal statement about the afterlife.
In sudden disgrace pictures a swift, complete reversal for the enemies, matching the sudden reversal in David's own confidence from verse 7 to verse 8.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 6:1-10. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. No sermon from the trusted cluster could be confirmed this session for Psalm 6, so sermon status is none.
